nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; adults found in lagoon and seaward reefs, aggregating in caves and under ledges at depths of 2–20 m, often hanging upside down inside caves

diet.notes: Generally advised to offer high-quality frozen meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine fish, squid, copepods, and frozen carnivore preparations. In the wild, has been documented feeding on small zooplanktonic crustaceans. Feeding at least twice daily is commonly recommended; some sources suggest frequent small feedings throughout the day. Has been reported to accept quality pellet or flake foods over time.

avoidWith: fish of similar shape and territory

imageCandidates.notes: Andrew J. Green / Reef Life Survey, 919x636. Sharp, well-lit in-situ shot of a single vibrant deep-blue Assessor macneilli, whole fish in frame against pink coralline reef; clearly shows the uniform dusky-blue coloration and rounded fins. Best available.
Rick Stuart-Smith / Reef Life Survey, 800x532. In-situ side profile of a single dark-blue individual, whole fish in frame; slightly duskier tone. Good confirming ID shot.
Donald Davesne, 2048x1536. Vivid royal-blue single specimen, whole fish in frame with good colour; overall image a touch soft from shooting through water, kept as a lower-ranked extra.

summary: A small, dusky-blue reef fish from Australia's Great Barrier Reef and New Caledonia, generally considered a good choice for beginners and reef aquariums. Known for swimming upside down inside caves, and tends to be peaceful toward most tankmates while remaining territorial toward other males of its kind.
careNotes:
The Macneill's Assessor Basslet (Assessor macneilli) is generally considered one of the more approachable small reef fish for beginner to intermediate marine aquarists. It tends to spend much of its time near caves and overhangs, often swimming upside down — a natural behavior adapted to its cave-dwelling lifestyle. Ample live rock with caves and crevices is widely regarded as essential for this species' wellbeing.

Temperament: Temperament designations vary across hobby sources. The general consensus is that it is peaceful toward most other species but becomes territorial toward conspecific males and fish of similar shape occupying comparable spaces in the tank. It is best kept singly or as a confirmed mated pair; multiple males in the same system are generally reported to be problematic.

Temperature note: Most hobby sources cite 72–78°F (22–26°C) as the target range, while wild habitat data from the Great Barrier Reef suggests water temperatures of 25–30°C are typical in nature. Most aquarists appear to keep them successfully at typical reef temperatures (24–26°C).

Reproduction: Males have been documented incubating egg masses in their mouths (paternal mouthbrooding) for approximately 15–16 days, which has been successfully reproduced in captivity. Captive-bred individuals are available from specialist producers. Captive-bred specimens generally acclimate more readily than wild-caught fish.

Cave-swimming behavior: This species routinely swims and rests upside down inside caves — this is normal and is an adaptation to their natural cave-dwelling habitat, not a sign of illness.

Health: Has been reported to be susceptible to common marine parasites such as ich and marine velvet; maintaining excellent water quality and minimizing stress are widely considered key preventive measures.

Tank size: Most hobby sources recommend a 30-gallon (approximately 113 L) minimum for a single specimen, with larger tanks generally better able to accommodate territorial behavior.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooding has been documented in the wild and in captivity; the male incubates the egg mass in his mouth for approximately 15–16 days. Captive breeding has been achieved, with ORA having produced captive-bred specimens. Specific breeding difficulty has not been well-documented in most hobby sources, but successful captive propagation has been reported.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Plesiopidae confirmed, correcting LiveAquaria's Grammidae), size (6.0 cm TL), native range (Great Barrier Reef and New Caledonia), and wild temperature data (25–30°C). Temperature disagreement with hobby sources documented in careNotes.

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (2.5–3 inches), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), tank size (30 gal), care level (Easy), temperament (Semi-Aggressive). Values largely consistent with LiveAquaria.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Additional data point: size 8 cm, temp 24–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.022–1.025, tank 75 L (20 gal), temperament peaceful, family Plesiopidae. Smaller tank recommendation of 75 L noted but 120 L used per err-larger rule.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Additional data point: temp 75–80°F (23.9–26.7°C), pH 7.7–8.5, salinity 1.020–1.026, tank 30 gal minimum. Describes temperament as peaceful with territorial tendencies toward other basslets. Care level beginner-appropriate.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; males incubate egg masses in the mouth for approximately 15–16 days. Captive breeding documented (ORA captive-bred specimens available). Specific difficulty rating is not well-established in the hobby literature.
